div.p: These will be short notes on the video tutorial by Sanjay Shakkottai.

section.p: First we have to discuss ODEs. For the ODE, dx_t = \underbrace {\mu (x_t, t)}_{\text {``drift term''}} \mathrm {d}t We have the following possible vector representation
This can be thought of as x_{t + h} - x_t \approx \mu (x_t, t) \cdot h + o(h)

header.h2: How does the pdf of the SDE evolve?
Reversing the OU process to generate images.
Tweedle's Formula to compte the drift of the reverse process.

div.p: Short blog / notes from Benjamin Eysenbach's tutorial at Reinforcement Learning and Decision Making

section.p: The talk starts discussing Ben's amazing paper DIAYN. An idea that has become incredibly popular in unsupervised / self-supervised RL is learning conditional policies. In Contrastive Reinforcement Learning, the learn a goal-conditioned policy and value function i.e. \pi _{\theta }(s \mid g), \quad Q_{\psi }(s, a, g) However, we are not limited to goals. In successor-feature-based frameworks, one learns a task vector \mathbf {w} such that r(s, a) = \phi (s,a) \cdot \mathbf {w}, where \phi is a lower dimensional representation. To learn the task vector (assuming you have \phi which is a whole other issue), you can recover \mathbf {w} by solving the linear regression \min _{\psi } \|r(s,a) - \phi (s,a) \cdot \mathbf {w}_{\psi }\|^{2} One can then learn a task conditioned policy \pi _{\theta }(s \mid \mathbf {w}). This is precisely the idea in USFA.

Getting back to DIAYN (get used to these tangents), instead of a task or goal, they condition on a skill \mathbf {z}. However, unlike a goal which is just a state in the environment, or a task which can be learned via regression as above, it is unclear how to learn this "skill" \mathbf {z}.
In this paper, they consider a skill as something distinguishable by a third party. What does this mean:

div.em: If you have a skill, then a discriminator observing you perform your skill should be able to tell you which skill you performed.

section.p:
Crucially, the discriminator does not know which actions you took when performing this skill. Namely, they must be able to identify the skill purely based on the states you visited when performing that skill. Mathematically, this can be roughly translated as maximizing the mutual information I^{\pi }(\mathbf {z}; \tau ), where \tau is a trajectory of states (s_0, s_1, \ldots , s_T) \sim \pi visited by your policy \pi . Using our previous knowledge of mutual information, we can rewrite this as I^{\pi }(\mathbf {z}, \tau ) = H(\mathbf {z}) - H(\mathbf {z} \mid \tau ), where H is the entropy. Since H(\mathbf {z}) is a constant, this is essentially, \min _{\mathbf {z}} H(\mathbf {z} \mid \tau ) which very intuitively corresponds to the notion that \mathbf {z} should be able to be predicted by \tau . Formally, this is captured by the fact that the entropy is minimized i.e. 0 when \mathbf {z} = f(\tau ) for some invertible f.
Recall that entropy is actually defined on the PDF of a random variable X. Therefore, minimizing the entropy over \mathbf {z} is actually finding a distribution p(\mathbf {z}).
To learn the distribution p(\mathbf {z}), they utilize the ELBO.

learning a discrimator distribution q_{\theta } such that \max _{\theta } q_{\theta }(\mathbf {z} \mid s_t), where we can easily learn this via maximum likelihood estimation because we are given s_{t + 1} \sim \pi (s_{t} \mid \mathbf {z})

div.p: I have been using LazyVim for some time now, but I have now run into issues multiple times where understanding how LazyVim is doing something is far more difficult than if I had written my own setup. I allocated one day for this adventure and really just wanted to make sure I had support for \TeX , python, forester, and C/C++. Due to my (self-imposed) time constraint, I do not have the associated resources linked for each of the things discussed below. At some point, I hope to come back and more thoroughly cover each of the components.
div.section: Sane Defaults To my surprise, a lot of the features that I had come to take for granted were actually options set up internally by Lazyvim. For example, I was shocked with 8 space indents!! and I could not even copy from one terminal instance to another... Due to this, I went and found all of the options I liked from Lazyvim and added them to my new configuration in configs/options.lua.

Installing a Plugin Manager For this, we will be using the defacto standard lazy.nvim. This is actually straightforward and kind of "just works". Just follow the installation guide in their documentation.

Setting up Auto Complete This is one of the main motivations for me making the switch. It seems nvim-cmp has finally been replaced with a new blink.cmp, so that is what we will be using.
Language Server Protocol It turns out there is a lot that goes into getting LSP setup correctly.
First we must actually install the language servers. To do this the easiest way, we use the mason.nvim and mason.nvim-lspconfig plugins. At some point, I might actually figure out how to set up lsp myself without lspconfig but that point is not now.
Through nvim-lspconfig, we can set up each of the servers we want to have LSP support. I just set up clangd, pyright, and texlab. This was a bit ridiculous. The first of many challenges was around import resolution in python. To remedy this, I needed to write a function to find the virtual environment directory and then set the pythonPath to the venv python binary. Previously, I think I was just using pylsp and installing it as a pip package to each python venv. I much prefer the new way, and I think pyright is overall a much better lsp.
Forester Completion Another necessary completion source for me is the one provided by forester.nvim. Similar to vimtex, the reference completion support is VERY useful. Obviously, I need completion when I am writing this blog!!! This was a little more involved. The first difficulty was that the completion source provided by the forester.nvim plugin was for nvim-cmp. It turns out this is a prevalent enough problem that the author of blink.cmp wrote an additional plugin blink.compat to allow for nvim-cmp completion sources. While this sounds all fine and good, nvim-compat expects plugins that return the completion source themselves, whereas in forester.nvim the completion source is just one submodule of a more feature-rich plugin. To get around this, I needed to look into the blink.compat code and find how they are registering the sources and just do it myself.

section.p: Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always been partial to snippets that auto-expand. I had them set up prior to Lazyvim but with Lazyvim I had resigned to using friendly-snippets with native nvim snippets. Since I was already redoing everything, this time around I decided not to compromise. Once upon a time (right when it came out I think?) I tried out Luasnips, but it seems that they now have far more extensive features. They are also natively supported by blink.cmp!! It feels necessary that I plug the awesome repo that ports the original Ultisnips snippets to Luasnip. With this, I was able to easily add my own forester snippets!!!

A fun little thing that I had been hoping to do for awhile and is finally now possible - I can load latex snippets when inside math environments in forester!!!! To do this, I looked into the forester treesitter grammar and found the corresponding nodetypes for math envs. It was then straightforward to detect whether we were in a math env and to load the associated latex snippets.

There is a very cool add-on to anki called AnkiConnect that has an associated plugin anki.nvim. Basically, AnkiConnects allows you to make requests to Anki and receive/send useful information from/to your decks. Unfortunately, anki.nvim built-in commands didn't seem all that useful to me. However, it provided the necessary infrastructure for me to accomplish my desired workflow.

Namely, I made my own command that queries Anki for the deck names, which you can then pick from using telescope pickers. When you select one it will create a new flashcard in a specified flashcard directory under a directory created based on the deck name. You can then send this card to that deck using the existing AnkiSend command.

I also continued the snippet fun here. When writing Anki cards, you can write latex code between [latex] [/latex] delimiters. I wrote a quick function to detect whether we are in these delimiters and if we are then to load the latex snippets from the previous section. I also added some basic anki filetype plugins to insert things like these delimiters.

div.section: Formatters and Linters compat.nvim, mason.nvim, black, isort.
